Mercedes C220 CDI / C220d Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes C220 CDI / C220d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned C220 engine typically costs £1,500–£4,500 supply only depending on generation. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500 depending on location and workshop rates. A replacement OM646 engine costs on average £2,160 in good condition, with a new or reconditioned engine costing up to £3,038. A replacement OM651 timing chain and tensioner costs around £700–£900. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes C220 CDI / C220d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on which engine generation your C220 has. The OM611 and OM646 (1997–2008) are widely regarded as 'bulletproof' diesels — 'will likely run forever'. The OM651 (2008–2018) has a known timing chain tensioner weakness — a £700 fix at a good indie. The OM654 (2018–present) has early reports of injector seal leaks and timing chain wear. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes C220 CDI / C220d?

acceptedAnswer.text: Injector 'black death' on OM611/OM646 engines — diesel leaks past injector seals and carbonises into black tar, timing chain tensioner wear on OM651 engines — causing cold-start rattle, turbocharger and boost pressure issues across all generations, and AdBlue system failures on OM651/OM654 (W205/W206).

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes C220 CDI / C220d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £3,000–£12,000 C-Class, a £2,500–£4,500 recon is 25–150% of the car's value. For a clean pre-2008 OM646 example, replacement is almost always worth it given the engine's legendary durability. For OM651 examples with documented chain work, replacement is viable. For examples under £2,000, the replacement cost may exceed our 70% Mercedes threshold — get a scrap valuation first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes C220 CDI / C220d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: W202/W203 (1997–2004) have OM611, W203 facelift/W204 early (2004–2008) have OM646, W204/W205 (2008–2018) have OM651, W205 facelift/W206 (2018–present) have OM654. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the OM651 timing chain, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A timing chain and tensioner replacement costs around £700–£900 at a good indie. Some owners have reported changing just the tensioner twice at high mileages without replacing the whole chain set. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. If the chain snaps, a full engine replacement is usually the only option.

eraMap.title: Mercedes C220 CDI / C220d — Which Engine Does Your Car Actually Have?

rows.eraNote: 2.1L four-cylinder — 125 hp. Early CDI, simple and robust. Injector "black death" is the main weak point

2.1L four-cylinder — 170–204 hp. Timing chain tensioner wear is a known weakness

2.0L four-cylinder with mild hybrid — 197–200 hp. Revised OM654, still emerging reliability data

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes C220 CDI / C220d Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a C220 engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). OM651 timing chain replacement is a significant job — some specialists quote around £700–£900.

problems.title: Injector "Black Death" (OM611 & OM646, W202 & W203)
problems.symptoms: Diesel/exhaust smell in the cabin, ticking or chuffing noise from the engine bay, black tar-like residue around injector bases, rough idle, hard starting
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you smell diesel or hear chuffing

problems.recommendation: "Black death" is a well-known issue on Mercedes CDI engines, particularly the OM611 and OM646. It occurs when the copper washer seal at the base of an injector fails, allowing combustion gases and diesel to escape. The escaping gases carbonise into a sticky black tar-like substance that is incredibly difficult to remove. If you smell diesel in the cabin or hear a chuffing noise from the engine, have the injector seals inspected immediately. A £200–£600 repair is far cheaper than the damage that can occur if left unaddressed.

failureLink.label: Read the full injector "black death" guide → /failures/injector-black-death/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Wear (OM651, W204 & W205)
problems.symptoms: Rattling or slapping noise from the engine on cold start, especially after the car has been sitting, metallic ticking that subsides as oil pressure builds

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The OM651 timing chain is a known weak spot. A timing chain and tensioner replacement is around £700 at a good indie, "its a known weak spot on the OM651 but really isn't the end of the world compared to some cars issues". Some owners have reported changing the tensioner twice — first at 290,000 km and again at 390,000 km — without needing to replace the whole chain set. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, get the tensioner inspected immediately. A £700–£1,000 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than a £4,500–£8,000 engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide → /failures/om651-timing-chain/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

problems.title: Turbocharger & Boost Pressure Issues (All Generations)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, limp mode (engine won't rev above 3,000 rpm), whistling or siren-like noise, black smoke, engine warning light
problems.repairCost: £800–£2,500 (turbocharger rebuild or replacement; boost hose repair)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you experience power loss or hear whistling

problems.recommendation: Turbocharger and boost pressure issues are common across all C220 generations. Common causes include jammed turbo vanes, blocked EGR valves, leaking boost hoses, and faulty boost pressure sensors. A leaking boost hose is "a common problem on this car". If you experience power loss or limp mode, have the turbo system and boost hoses inspected immediately. A £800–£2,500 repair is far cheaper than the engine damage that can follow if ignored.

failureLink.label: Read the full turbo failure guide

problems.title: AdBlue & SCR System Issues (OM651 & OM654, W205 & W206)
problems.symptoms: "Starts not possible in XXX miles" countdown warning, engine management light, reduced power, AdBlue warning messages
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — AdBlue system issues are common on modern Mercedes diesels
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if countdown warning appears

problems.recommendation: On Mercedes OM651 and OM654 diesel engines, AdBlue faults are rarely caused by a single failed part. Common symptoms include countdown warnings and engine management lights. AdBlue issues can be "difficult to diagnose and expensive to fix". If you see an AdBlue warning or countdown, take the car to a trusted independent specialist with genuine MB STAR diagnostic equipment. Generic OBDII scanners won't help here.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: C220 CDI / C220d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — tensioner replacement is a known fix
Full engine replacement is the only reliable fix
Catch early to prevent engine damage

buyingChecks.title: Buying This C220 CDI / C220d? Check These First
buyIf: * Pre-2008 OM646-engined car (no DPF, bulletproof reliability) — "will likely run forever"
* Documented timing chain work on any OM651-engined car (2008–2018)
* Full service history with regular oil changes — the OM651 is unforgiving of neglect
* Clean injector bases with no black residue (OM611/OM646)
avoidIf: * OM651-engined car with cold-start rattle and no timing chain history — this is a ticking time bomb
* OM611/OM646 with injector "black death" and no documented repair
* Poor service history — diesel Mercedes are reliable but need proper maintenance
* AdBlue countdown warnings with no diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The C220 CDI/C220d badge spans four very different diesel engine generations. The OM611 and OM646 (1997–2008) are widely regarded as "bulletproof" diesels — simple, robust and "likely to run forever". The OM651 (2008–2018) introduced timing chain tensioner wear as a known weakness — a £700 fix at a good indie. The OM654 (2018–present) has early reports of injector and timing chain concerns. Buy a pre-2008 OM646 if you want the most durable option. Buy a well-maintained OM651 with documented chain work if you want newer refinement. Buy on documented history, not on generation alone.

cta.label: → Compare C220 CDI / C220d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes C220 CDI or C220d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or slapping noise (timing chain tensioner warning — OM651 critical)
* Injector Bases — Remove engine cover and check for black tar-like residue ("black death" — OM611/OM646)
* Service History — Including which engine era (OM611, OM646, OM651 or OM654) and confirmed oil-interval discipline
* Turbo Health — Listen for whistling; check for power loss or limp mode
* AdBlue System — Any countdown warnings or engine management lights? (W205/W206 OM651/OM654)
* DPF Health — Check for DPF warning lights; short journeys are a red flag (post-2008 models)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Look for timing, injector or boost-related codes
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly.
